coredata.dc:title: Novel high-hydrophilic carbon dots from petroleum coke for boosting injection pressure reduction and enhancing oil recovery
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yining
Cao, Mengjiao
Zhao, Qingshan
Wu, Xiaocui
Guo, Fang
Tang, Lisha
Tan, Xiaojie
Wu, Wenting
Shi, Yufan
Dai, Caili
coredata.dc:description: Zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials show intriguing potential in the field of unconventional oil resource development for reducing injection pressure and enhancing oil recovery. However, the complicated synthesis procedure, necessity for surfactant and limited understanding of the mechanism impede their practical applications. In this study, novel high-hydrophilic carbon dots (hh-CDs) with an average particle size of 2.54 ± 0.016 nm were facilely synthesized through an electrochemical approach by employing cost-effective petroleum coke as the carbon source, followed by treating with ozone for further oxidation. The abundant surface functional groups render hh-CDs superior hydrophilicity, dispersibility and stability. Core flooding tests show 0.20 wt% hh-CDs nanofluid delivers a prominent pressure-reducing rate of 23.81% and an enhancement in oil recovery of 26.38% without any surfactant. Analytical results of atomic force microscope (AFM) with hydrophobic probe reveal hh-CDs can adsorb on the rock surface to alter the micro-scale wettability from oil-wet to homogeneous water-wet. The hh-CDs can also efficiently reduce the core surface roughness and afford excess disjoining pressure for oil displacement, accounting for the impressive performance. This work provides a feasible oil systemic circulation for the synthesis of versatile surfactant-free carbon dots for boosting injection pressure reduction and enhancing oil recovery in tight oil reservoirs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Petroleum coke
Electrochemical approach
Injection pressure reduction
Enhanced oil recovery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, HR-TEM (inset) images and corresponding statistics of particle distribution of CDs (a) and hh-CDs (b). AFM 2D (c) and 3D (d) images of hh-CDs. The width and height profiles along the line in the 2D image (e).
Full range XPS spectra of CDs and hh-CDs (a). C 1s XPS spectra of CDs (c) and hh-CDs (d).
The contact angle of CDs (a) and hh-CDs (b) nanofluid droplets on hydrophilic glass surface.
Variation of the injecting pressure during deionized water, CDs nanofluid and hh-CDs nanofluid flooding.
The surface adhesion map of core before (a) and after (b) hh-CDs adsorption. Frequency distribution histogram of the adhesion force before (c) and after (d) hh-CDs adsorption.
Topographic image of the core before(a) and after (b) the hh-CDs adsorption.
Change of oil contact angle with time in the hh-CDs nanofluid.
Illustration for the synthesis of hh-CDs.
Apparatus of the physical simulation experiment.
Core flooding results of deionized water, CDs nanofluid and hh-CDs nanofluid.
Core flooding results of hh-CDs nanofluids with different concentration.

head.ce:title: Novel high-hydrophilic carbon dots from petroleum coke for boosting injection pressure reduction and enhancing oil recovery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oilfeld Chemistry, School of Petroleum Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oilfeld Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Key Laboratory of Oilfeld Chemistry, School of Petroleum Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petroleum Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu, 610599, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Petroleum Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610599, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials show intriguing potential in the field of unconventional oil resource development for reducing injection pressure and enhancing oil recovery. However, the complicated synthesis procedure, necessity for surfactant and limited understanding of the mechanism impede their practical applications. In this study, novel high-hydrophilic carbon dots (hh-CDs) with an average particle size of 2.54 ± 0.016 nm were facilely synthesized through an electrochemical approach by employing cost-effective petroleum coke as the carbon source, followed by treating with ozone for further oxidation. The abundant surface functional groups render hh-CDs superior hydrophilicity, dispersibility and stability. Core flooding tests show 0.20 wt% hh-CDs nanofluid delivers a prominent pressure-reducing rate of 23.81% and an enhancement in oil recovery of 26.38% without any surfactant. Analytical results of atomic force microscope (AFM) with hydrophobic probe reveal hh-CDs can adsorb on the rock surface to alter the micro-scale wettability from oil-wet to homogeneous water-wet. The hh-CDs can also efficiently reduce the core surface roughness and afford excess disjoining pressure for oil displacement, accounting for the impressive performance. This work provides a feasible oil systemic circulation for the synthesis of versatile surfactant-free carbon dots for boosting injection pressure reduction and enhancing oil recovery in tight oil reservoirs.

ce:section.ce:para: The unconventional oil resource such as tight oil and shale oil owns the properties of micro-nano pore throats, small porosity, extremely low permeability and serious heterogeneity, which brings great challenges to its high-efficient development [1]. The main method to develop unconventional oil at home and abroad is depletion, whereas the production decreases rapidly and the recovery ratio is relatively low. In order to increase the production, the formation energy is generally replenished by water injection. However, water injection will lead to hydration expansion and scaling of clay and other substances on the rock surface near the well zone, resulting in throat blockage, which further causes underpressure, underinjection, high water injection pressure and serious shortage of water injection [2]. Therefore, decreasing the water-injection pressure and enhancing the oil recovery is urgently needed in tight oil development.

Nanomaterial injection-increasing technology is a technology to reduce water injection pressure and improve water injection rate by adsorption of nanomaterials on the surface of formation rocks to reduce the flow resistance and increase oil recovery [3]. Nanomaterials such as nano-SiO2, TiO2, Al2O3, etc. have been widely investigated for this purpose, among which nano-SiO2 has attracted the most attention [4–6]. Early in 1966, SiO2 nanomaterials was first applied in Russia in case of the Roman Shijin oilfield water injection wells. Zhang et al. [7] reported super-hydrophobic SiO2 nanoparticles with the assistant of surfactant can be adsorbed on rock surface to increase effective permeability and reduce the injection pressure of low permeability reservoir. Ju et al. [8] demonstrated hydrophilic polysilicon nanoparticles can change the wettability of reservoir rock from hydrophobic to hydrophilic for enhanced oil recovery. However, SiO2 nanomaterials suffer from relatively large size (compared to the nanoscale pore-throat units), high density and prone to aggregation blockage, seriously limiting their practical application scope.

Owing to the remarkable physical and chemical properties, as well as controllable morphology, abundant resource and environmentally friendly, carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted increasing attention as alternatives to the traditional nanomaterials for application in tight oil development [9–11]. The one-dimensional carbon nanotubes [9] and two-dimensional graphene nanomaterials [12,13] were firstly investigated as effective promoters for injection pressure reduction and improving oil sweep efficiency. By comparison, zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials stand out to match with the micro-nano pores and roars for their unique nanosize effect. Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of emerging carbon-based nanoparticles with diameters of less than 10 nm, which have been widely investigated in areas of optoelectronics, luminescence, sensors and photocatalysis due to their non-toxic, strong fluorescence and excellent electron transfer ability [14–16]. Remarkably, the excellent dispersity, easy surface modification and ultrasmall size feature render CDs intriguing potential in tight oil or shale oil development. Most recently, Diaz and Kellici et al. [17]. reported a continuous hydrothermal flow synthesis method to fabricate S-functionalized carbon quantum dots, which showed enhanced oil recovery of 17% by changing the carbonate rocks wettability towards water wet and creating temporary log-jamming. Our group [18] prepared hydrophobic carbon nanoparticles nanofluid with the aid of Tween-80 and showed an excellent pressure reducing rate of 17%. Nevertheless, the complicated synthesis procedure, necessity for surfactants and limited understanding of the mechanism impede the further development and application of CDs.

Petroleum coke is one of the main by-products in the petroleum refining industry [19,20]. With the increasing tension and inferior quality of crude oil resources, the production of petroleum coke has increased constantly in recent years, urgently calling for effective and value-added utilization of the industrial waste [21]. On account of the high content of carbon and low content of ash, petroleum coke has been employed to synthesize carbon materials and applied in diverse fields such as CO2 capture, water treatment, energy storage and electrocatalysis [22–24]. The physicochemical properties and abundant resources endow petroleum coke a promising precursor for massive construction of functional CDs towards tight oil development, opening up a feasible avenue for the valuable utilization and systemic circulation.

In the present study, by employing cost-effective petroleum coke as the carbon source, novel high-hydrophilic carbon dots (hh-CDs) were facilely synthesized through an electrochemical approach, followed by treating with ozone for further oxidation. The abundant surface functional groups make hh-CDs excellent hydrophilicity, dispersibility and stability. Core flooding tests were conducted, and the 0.20 wt% hh-CDs nanofluid exhibited remarkable pressure-reducing and oil recovery performance without the addition of any surfactant. Especially, by using atomic force microscope (AFM) with hydrophobic probe the pressure reduction and enhanced oil recovery mechanism for tight oil development were detailed investigated and revealed.

Petroleum coke and petroleum asphalt were derived from China National Petroleum Corporation. Ammonium hydroxide aqueous solution (25–28%), ethanol, dodecyl mercaptan and dichloromethane were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Inorganic filter membrane (0.22 μm) was purchased from Shanghai Xingya Purification Material Co. LTD. Cores with a permeability of 5 mD were purchased from Hai'an Petroleum Scientific Research Instrument Co., Ltd. Dehydrated crude oil was mixed with kerosene in a ratio of 1:7 to produce a simulated oil with a viscosity of 9 mPa s. Ultrapure water required for the experiments was prepared by a reverse osmosis unit (ULUPURE, UPT–II–5T).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of petroleum coke-based CDs and hh-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Petroleum coke-based CDs was firstly fabricated through an electrochemical approach, as shown in Scheme 1 . Typically, 20.0 g petroleum coke (as the carbon source) and 5.0 g petroleum pitch (as the binder) were added into 30 mL dichloromethane. After evaporating the solvent, the paste was moulded into discoid precursors by hot-compaction treatment, and disk electrodes were obtained through calcinating at 800 °C for 2 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, two pieces of the prepared electrodes were inserted into 150 mL 5 wt% ammonia solution as the working electrode and counter electrode. Static potential of 30 V was applied to the system using a direct current (DC) power. After the anode disk electrode was corroded completely, the mixture was filtered and neutralized by evaporating the excess ammonia under 40 °C to obtain a dark-yellow CDs solution. A commercial ozone generator (EXT120T, Longevity Resources Co. Ltd., Canada) was used for ozonation at room temperature. The volume flow of oxygen was set at 500 mL min−1, and the ozone concentration output was set at 7.9 μg/L. The generated ozone was introduced into the CDs solution for 5 min to obtain hh-CDs. The solution was further diluted to certain concentrations (0.01–0.3 wt%) to obtain hh-CDs nanofluids.

The morphology and microstructure of the samples were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL-2100F) operated at 200 kV. The size/height distribution of hh-CDs, as well as the morphology and adhesion force mapping of hh-CDs adsorption on the core surface were measured by atomic force microscope (AFM, MultiMode8 SPM). The detailed modification of AFM tip is described in the Supplementary Material (Fig. S1). The surface functional groups of CDs and hh-CDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) recorded on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi) analysis was measured by an Mg Kɑ (1486.6 eV) monochromatic X-ray source. The contact angle was conducted on a contact angle measuring instrument (JC2000D2).

The core flooding tests were conducted and illustrated in Scheme 2 . The pore volume of dried cores was measured by porosity meter. After vacuum drying, the cores were saturated with simulated formation water. Then water was injected into the core at a constant flow rate, and the injection pressure was recorded to obtain the core permeability. After that, the core was saturated with simulated oil at constant speed and aged for 2 days at 90 °C. The simulated water, CDs nanofluid and hh-CDs nanofluids with different concentration were injected into the cores (5 cm × 2.5 cm) with permeability of 50 mD at a constant rate of 0.5 mL min−1, respectively. Meanwhile, the pressure drop was recorded until no oil was detected in the output liquid. The oil volume at the outlet was collected, and the recovery factor was calculated.

The morphology of the petroleum coke-based CDs and hh-CDs were firstly observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images. As shown in Fig. 1 a–b, CDs and hh-CDs are quasi-sphere and monodispersed without any agglomeration. The HR-TEM images reveal CDs and hh-CDs both show a clear lattice spacing of ∼0.216 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite (Fig. 1a–b, insets), suggesting the high crystallinity of the samples [25]. Further statistical analysis demonstrates similar average particle size and narrow distribution of 2.29 ± 0.028 nm and 2.54 ± 0.016 nm for CDs and hh-CDs, respectively. The slightly increased particle size of hh-CDs should be attributed to the introduction of more surface functional groups though the ozonation process. The microstructure of hh-CDs was further confirmed by atomic force microscope (AFM). The AFM 2D and 3D images display the excellent distribution and no obvious agglomeration of hh-CDs (Fig. 1c–d). Further analysis reveals the topographic height of hh-CDs is in a range of 1.5–3.0 nm with an average height of around 2.5 nm (Fig. 1e), which is in good agreement with the size distribution measured by the TEM tests.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the pristine CDs and hh-CDs were investigated to examine the surface functional groups, as shown in Fig. S2. As to the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, the typical peak at 3440 cm−1 assigned to –OH and the peak at 1635 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of CO imply the in-situ formation of oxygen-containing groups in the electrochemical approach [26,27]. Moreover, the peak observed at 3202 cm−1 is considered as the N–H stretching vibrations [28], and the peak at 1447 cm−1 corresponds to the CN/C–N in plane stretching vibrations of amine groups [29], indicating the simultaneous introduction of nitrogen-containing groups originated from the ammonium hydroxide electrolyte. Comparison study reveals the similar functional groups of hh-CDs with CDs, and the intensive absorption of the oxygen-containing groups suggests further oxidation of CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was further performed to confirm the functional groups of CDs and hh-CDs. As shown in the full range XPS spectra (Fig. 2 a), both CDs and hh-CDs contain C, N and O elements in the samples, and hh-CDs show an obvious higher content of oxygen than that of CDs. The C 1s XPS spectra of CDs and hh-CDs (Fig. 2b–c) can be deconvolved into five characteristic peaks corresponding to CC/C–C (284.17 eV), C–N (284.87 eV), C–O (286.27 eV), CO/CN (287.67 eV) and O–CO (288.58 eV), respectively [30]. As shown in Table S1, after ozonation, the percentage of CC/C–C and C–N decreases from 29.54% to 18.62%–21.15% and 17.58%, respectively, indicating a further surface oxidation of CDs through treating with ozone. Meanwhile, the percentage of C–O and O–CO increases from 9.88% to 14.88%–11.64% and 26.80%, respectively. Further study on the N 1s XPS spectra indicates a slight decrease of CN and N–H species and a percent increase of N–O from 2.75% to 4.06%, revealing partially further oxidation of the nitrogen-containing groups (Table S1 and Fig. S3). The above results illustrate the successful exfoliation of petroleum coke to synthesize CDs with abundant oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups through the electrochemical approach, and more oxygen-containing functional groups such as carboxyl and oxidized N are introduced in the following ozonation process. These abundant hydrophilic functional groups would further promote the hydrophilicity and dispersibility of hh-CDs.

The contact angle images of CDs and hh-CDs (0.2 wt% solution) droplets on the glass were shown in Fig. 3 . It is well known that the glass surface is hydrophilic, and the CDs and hh-CDs droplets show contact angles of 49° and 32°, respectively, revealing their hydrophilic characteristic. The smaller wetting angle of hh-CDs suggests its enhanced hydrophilicity induced by the intercalated oxygen-containing groups, which is consistent with the above FT-IR and XPS results. Fig. S4 depicts the hh-CDs nanofluid at different reserve times. As can be seen, the prepared hh-CDs nanofluid exhibit a remarkable aqueous dispersibility and durable stability, which should be beneficial from the abundant surface functional groups, exhibiting great potential for application in the field of tight oil development.

Oil-saturated cores with low permeability of ∼25 × 10−3 μm2 were employed for the core flooding experiments to evaluate the performance of prepared nanomaterials in tight oil development. Deionized water, CDs nanofluid (0.2 wt%) and hh-CDs nanofluid (0.2 wt%) were injected into core 1#, 2# and 3# at a low rate of 0.3 mL min−1, respectively. The variation trends of the injection pressure are recorded in Fig. 4 , and the recovery factor and pressure drop are shown in Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 4, as to the core flooding process of deionized water, the injection pressure firstly shows a sharp rise to a maximum value of 930.23 kPa, followed by a rapid fall. The oil in the core is continuously driven out in this process. Due to the water breakthrough capillary force, the injection pressure stabilizes at 479.38 kPa when 3.8 PV water is injected, with no more oil dropping out at the outlet. The final oil recovery rate is calculated to be 46.70%. In contrast, the injection pressure of CDs and hh-CDs nanofluid flooding discloses basically the same trend, which show a gentle initial slope and followed by a gradual decrease. The injection pressure of CDs nanofluid and hh-CDs nanofluid stabilizes at 398.23 kPa and 365.23 kPa, affording oil recovery rate of 67.14% and 70.74%, respectively. Therefore, compared with water flooding, both the pristine CDs nanofluid and hh-CDs nanofluid can efficiently reduce the injection pressure and increase the oil recovery. Remarkably, the hh-CDs nanofluid shows a superior pressure-reducing rate of 23.81% and enhanced oil recovery of 26.38% without the addition of any surfactant.

The influence of hh-CDs concentration on the oil displacement and depressurization performance was further investigated. Table 2 shows the experimental results of hh-CDs nanofluids with concentrations of 0.01 wt%, 0.10 wt%, 0.20 wt% and 0.30 wt%, respectively. Initially, with the increase of the concentration, both higher oil recovery factor and pressure-reducing rate are achieved. The 0.20 wt% hh-CDs nanofluid affords a remarkable pressure-reducing rate of 23.81% and enhanced oil recovery of 26.38%. The 0.30 wt% hh-CDs nanofluid gives a recovery factor of 71.23%, while the pressure-reducing rate drops to 14.58%. Therefore, when the nanofluid concentration is low, the hh-CDs nanoparticles are not able to form a dense adsorption layer on the core wall and affect the oil displacement process. While excessive concentration of hh-CDs would cause the blockage of small pore-throat units, resulting in an increased injection pressure. Notably, the superior recovery factor of 0.30 wt% hh-CDs nanofluid implies the effective interaction of hh-CDs on the oil-water interface to accelerate the displacement of reservoir oil. Considering the cost, sweep efficiency and depressurization rate, hh-CDs nanofluid with concentration of 0.20 wt% stands out.

To shed light on the enhancement mechanism of hh-CDs nanofluid, AFM with hydrophobic probe was employed to determine the micro-wettability before/after the adsorption of hh-CDs. A two-dimensional array of force curves is obtained by using hydrophobic AFM tip in deionized water, which can be demonstrated as a map of surface adhesion [31]. Force-separation curves are recorded in each internal domain of 100 nm to illustrate the adhesion force map in a 3 × 3 μm2 region. Generally, the adhesion is associated with surface interactions, including van der Waals’ (VDL) interaction, electrical double layer (EDL) interaction and possibly hydrophobic interaction. In this experiment, the contribution of VDL and EDL interactions do not change before and after hh-CDs adsorption, and the change in adhesion force corresponds with the hydrophobic interaction. The adhesion maps of core surface before and after hh-CDs adsorption are shown in Fig. 5 . Due to hydrophobic effect, the adhesion of AFM tip in hydrophobic region is much higher than that of hydrophilic region. The white region in the adhesion map signifies high adhesion, corresponding to the hydrophobic domain. While the black region is assigned to low adhesion and corresponds to the hydrophilic domain. The white and black regions in the original core surface adhesion force map are unevenly distributed, indicating the heterogeneous hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of the core surface (Fig. 5a). This should be caused by the uneven distribution of minerals and roughness of core surface. After immersion in the hh-CDs nanofluid, the core surface shows a much weaker adhesion over the scanned region, implying the high hydrophilicity due to the adsorption of hh-CDs (Fig. 5b).

The histograms of adhesion force frequency distribution on the core surface before/after hh-CDs adsorption are shown in Fig. 5c–d. The adhesion force on the original core surface ranges from 0 to ∼9.50 nN, centered at 4.30 nN. After hh-CDs adsorption, the adhesion distribution becomes quite narrow, ranging from 0 to 2.80 nN, with the peak centered at 1.37 nN. It is obvious that the wettability of the core surface changes from a heterogeneous character to a homogenous distribution after hh-CDs adsorption, and the adhesion force on the core surface is reduced by about 3.14 times. The significant decrease of adhesion force suggests that hh-CDs change the chemical characteristics of core surface, presenting a strong hydrophilic core surface. The change of wettability will affect the adhesion energy between oil droplets and rock surface, which will directly affect the oil displacement efficiency. When the pore throat units of oil-wet reservoir are scoured by hh-CDs nanofluid, the nanoparticles are absorbed, altering the micro-scale wettability from oil-wet to homogeneous water-wet. At this point, the adhesion energy between oil and core is significantly reduced, which makes the oil droplets more likely to strip off from the core [17].

The morphology of core surface (5∗5 μm) before/after hh-CDs adsorption was examined and shown in Fig. 6 . The core surface of original pore throats is uneven, demonstrating a root mean square (RMS) roughness of ∼73 nm. An adsorption layer of nanoparticles can be obviously seen on the core surface after hh-CDs adsorption, and the RMS roughness of the surface decreases to ∼41 nm, with a reduction rate of 43.8%. The injected water flows through the rough wall surface when water flooding. Since the energy loss of water is large, a large resistance coefficient is resulted. In contrast, in the process of hh-CDs nanofluid flooding, hh-CDs not only fill the large pore throat units, but also form an adsorption layer on the wall surface. This result leads to smoother seepage channel, significantly reducing the critical layer thickness and flow resistance [32].

To reveal the influence of hh-CDs nanofluid on the oil displacement process, the variation of oil contact angle on solid-liquid interfaces with time was investigated. Simulated oil was placed on the core surface saturated with oil and inverted in the hh-CDs nanofluid, and the oil contact angle was measured every 30 min. As shown in Fig. 7 , the initial oil contact angle of the oil-wet core piece is ∼30°, which gradually increases with the adsorption time and reaches ∼53° at 12 h and ∼83° at 24 h, indicating the wettability of core changed from oil-wet to neutral-wet. Therefore, the hh-CDs nanofluid owns excellent ability for interfacial modification, which alters the core surface from hydrophobic to hydrophilic, in accord with the above AFM results. The unique nanostructure and physicochemical properties render hh-CDs an excellent interfacial activity to form an oil/hh-CDs nanofluid/core three-phase contact region. Due to Brownian motion of hh-CDs and electrostatic repulsion between the nanoparticles, the high hydrophilic nanoparticles would induce structural separation pressure [18,33], providing excess disjoining interaction to strip off the oil droplet and resulting in excellent oil displacement performance.

The above results demonstrate that the hh-CDs nanofluid with optimized concentration of 0.20 wt% can effectively reduce the injection pressure and enhance the oil recovery. The abundant surface functional groups render hh-CDs superior hydrophilicity, dispersibility and stability. When the hh-CDs nanofluid is injected into the core, hh-CDs can adsorb on the rock wall and change the wettability of surface from oil-wet to water-wet, making it easier for oil droplets to desorb from the solid surface owing to excess disjoining pressure. The hydrophilic core surface can also efficiently reduce the adhesion force between oil and rock surface in the transportation process. Meanwhile, the RMS roughness of core surface is reduced by 43.8%, significantly reducing the critical layer thickness and flow resistance. Owing to the versatile interaction of hh-CDs nanofluid, prominent injection pressure reduction and enhanced oil recovery are achieved.

In conclusion, we demonstrate an electrochemical and ozonation approach to fabricate novel hh-CDs with an average particle size of 2.54 ± 0.016 nm by employing cost-effective petroleum coke as the carbon source. The abundant surface functional groups render hh-CDs superior hydrophilicity, dispersibility and stability. The hh-CDs can efficiently adsorb on the rock surface to alter the wettability from oil-wet to water-wet, significantly reduce the core surface roughness of 43.8% and adhesion force of 3.14 times, as well as afford excess disjoining pressure to make oil displace from a solid surface. The 0.20 wt% hh-CDs nanofluid delivers a prominent pressure-reducing rate of 23.81% and enhanced oil recovery of 26.38% without the addition of any surfactant. This work provides a feasible oil systemic circulation for the synthesis of versatile surfactant-free carbon dots for boosting injection pressure reduction and enhancing oil recovery in tight oil reservoirs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2019YFA0708700), China; the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. ZR2019QB016), China; the Fundamental Research Fund for the Central Universities (No. 18CX02015A), China; the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51704313), China.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.08.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in improved/enhanced oil recovery technologies for tight and shale reservoirs
Fuel

Chem. Eng. Sci.

ChemNanoMat

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of silicon quantum dots based nano-fluid for enhanced oil recovery in tight Bakken cores
Fuel

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced heavy oil recovery in sandstone cores using TiO2 nanofluids
